chunks.page_summary: This page provides essential guidance on how to clean Rheonics inline viscometer and density sensor probes, highlighting the importance of cleanliness for accurate measurements and outlining recommended cleaning practices, including warnings against damaging methods.
product_names: inline fluid viscometer and density meter
chunks.extracted_text:
Phone: +1 713 234 0863

How to clean your Rheonics probe?
Modified on: Thu, 21 Dec, 2023 at 4:43 PM

Which products are involved?
Inline process viscometer SRV (https://rheonics.com/products/inline-viscometer-srv/)
inline process density meter SRD (https://rheonics.com/products/inline-density-meter-srd/)
Visit Rheonics product page: Products » inline fluid viscometer and density meter (https://rheonics.com/products/)

1. What is the importance of a clean probe?
Accurate measurements of process fluid density and viscosity are an important part of many industrial processes, and cleanliness plays a major role in obtaining accurate readings. Often the process fluid causes deposits and measurement probes should be cleaned to ensure accurate viscosity and density measurements.

Dirt, debris, sticking material, and other contaminants can interfere with the sensor operation and cause incorrect readings. The SR probes are a stable, robust sensor system that operates over a wide range of temperatures and pressures. The standard sensor is made of 316L stainless steel, which is one of the most widely used materials in manufacturing processes involving fluids.

The SRV and SRD sensors are based on a resonant sensing element that operates in the range of approximately 7-8 kHz. The sensor measures fluid properties in a layer of fluid surrounding the resonant element. The sensor measures what it is in contact with so it is important to ensure that fluid that is of interest is in contact with the sensing element at all times.

If the sensor sensing element gets coated with sticky fluid and solids, it is recommended that the sensor is cleaned. This cleaning often takes place in situ aka the sensor is cleaned along with the line or vessel it is installed in. However, at times it is desired or important to remove and mechanically clean the probe/sensing element.

Adherent films can be removed by using appropriate solvents and/or wiping with a cloth or paper. Under no circumstances should abrasives be used to clean the resonator, as these will change the geometry and destroy its calibration.

Do not use ultrasonic cleaners under any circumstance as that could damage the probe internal components.
key_facts: Cleanliness is crucial for obtaining accurate viscosity and density measurements from Rheonics probes.
Process fluid can cause deposits on measurement probes, necessitating cleaning to ensure accurate readings.
Dirt, debris, sticking material, and other contaminants can interfere with sensor operation and lead to incorrect readings.
Rheonics SR probes are stable, robust sensor systems capable of operating across a wide range of temperatures and pressures.
The standard SR sensor is constructed from 316L stainless steel, a widely used material in manufacturing processes involving fluids.
SRV and SRD sensors employ a resonant sensing element that operates at approximately 7-8 kHz.
The sensor measures fluid properties within a layer of fluid immediately surrounding the resonant element.
It is important to ensure that the fluid of interest is consistently in contact with the sensing element.
Cleaning of the sensor is recommended when its sensing element becomes coated with sticky fluid and solids.
Cleaning can occur 'in situ' (with the sensor installed in the line or vessel) or by removing the probe for mechanical cleaning.
Adherent films on the probe can be removed using appropriate solvents and/or wiping with a cloth or paper.
warnings_notes: Under no circumstances should abrasives be used to clean the resonator, as these will change the geometry and destroy its calibration.
Do not use ultrasonic cleaners under any circumstance as that could damage the probe internal components.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides instructions for cleaning Rheonics SRV/SRD probes and discusses the chemical compatibility of Stainless steel 316, listing types of chemical hazards.
chunks.extracted_text:
2. Removing the coating with a solvent-saturated cloth or paper

Cleaning SR (SRV or SRD) probes requires a gentle hand and the right materials. To start, use a soft cloth to wipe down the SR probe thoroughly. Make sure to remove any grease or dirt buildup that may have accumulated on its surface. Then, dampen a clean cloth with warm water and mild dish soap. Gently wipe down the stainless steel probe with the cloth and rinse it off with clean water. Finally, dry the stainless steel probe with a soft cloth. With these steps, your SR probe will remain clean and perform its best.

Figure 1. Manually wiping the SRV probe with a cloth

When it comes to stainless steel probes, it's important to keep them clean in order to protect the material from corrosive elements. Washing stainless steel probes with warm water and mild detergent can help prevent corrosion. For tougher soils or stains, a stainless steel cleaner or vinegar-based solution can be used on the probes. It is important to remember not to use any harsh abrasives, metallic scouring pads, or steel wool as these can damage stainless steel surfaces.

3. Chemical compatibility for Stainless steel 316

When it comes to chemically cleaning the Rheonics sensors, there are many different chemical solutions available. The most popular solution is a mixture of water and detergent, which can help remove dirt, dust, and other foreign materials from the stainless steel surface. It is important to note that stainless steel should not be exposed to harsh chemical cleaners for long periods of time, as this can cause damage to the surface. Additionally, stainless steel should always be dried thoroughly after being chemically cleaned in order to avoid any corrosion.

The following table can be used to verify the compatibility with different chemicals:

A: Asphyxiant (Gas and vapor only)
C: Corrosive
F: Flammable
O: Oxidizer
T: Toxic
key_facts: SR (SRV or SRD) probes should be cleaned with a soft cloth, warm water, and mild dish soap to remove grease or dirt buildup.
For tougher soils or stains on stainless steel probes, a stainless steel cleaner or vinegar-based solution can be used.
Stainless steel probes must be kept clean to protect them from corrosive elements and prevent corrosion.
The most popular chemical cleaning solution for Rheonics sensors is a mixture of water and detergent.
Stainless steel should always be dried thoroughly after chemical cleaning to avoid corrosion.
Chemical compatibility for Stainless steel 316 needs to be verified.
Asphyxiant (Gas and vapor only), Corrosive, Flammable, Oxidizer, and Toxic are categories used to verify chemical compatibility.
diagram_labels: Figure 1. Manually wiping the SRV probe with a cloth
warnings_notes: It is important to remember not to use any harsh abrasives, metallic scouring pads, or steel wool as these can damage stainless steel surfaces.
Stainless steel should not be exposed to harsh chemical cleaners for long periods of time, as this can cause damage to the surface.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides a chemical compatibility chart for various acids, detailing their potential effects and recommended usage guidelines.
chunks.extracted_text:
Hazard	Compatibility
Acids	
Acetic	C	No noticeable effect at low concentrations and room temperature. Moderate to severe effect at high concentration and/or high temperature. Test before using and verify calibration periodically.
Aqua Regia	C	Severe effect or degradation, exposure not recommended
Chromic	C	Minor effect or a slight change in appearance or properties. Test before repeated exposure and verify calibration periodically.
Hydrochloric	C	Severe effect or degradation, exposure not recommended
Hydrofluoric	C	Minor effect or a slight change in appearance or properties. Test before repeated exposure and verify calibration periodically.
Nitric	C	No noticeable effect
Phosphoric	C	Severe effect or degradation, exposure not recommended
Sulfuric	C	Severe effect or degradation, exposure not recommended
key_facts: Acetic acid shows no noticeable effect at low concentrations and room temperature, but a moderate to severe effect at high concentrations and/or high temperatures.
Aqua Regia causes severe effect or degradation.
Chromic acid may cause a minor effect or a slight change in appearance or properties.
Hydrochloric acid causes severe effect or degradation.
Hydrofluoric acid may cause a minor effect or a slight change in appearance or properties.
Nitric acid has no noticeable effect.
Phosphoric acid causes severe effect or degradation.
Sulfuric acid causes severe effect or degradation.
warnings_notes: For Acetic acid, test before using and verify calibration periodically, especially at high concentrations and/or temperatures.
Exposure to Aqua Regia is not recommended due to severe effect or degradation.
For Chromic acid, test before repeated exposure and verify calibration periodically.
Exposure to Hydrochloric acid is not recommended due to severe effect or degradation.
For Hydrofluoric acid, test before repeated exposure and verify calibration periodically.
Exposure to Phosphoric acid is not recommended due to severe effect or degradation.
Exposure to Sulfuric acid is not recommended due to severe effect or degradation.

chunks.page_summary: This page is a chemical compatibility table for various caustics, gases, and oxidants, detailing their effects on a material, likely related to Rheonics' viscometers or density sensors.
chunks.extracted_text:
Caustics
  Ammonium Hydroxide | C | No noticeable effect
  Potassium Hydroxide | C | No noticeable effect
  Sodium Hydroxide | C | Minor effect or a slight change in appearance or properties. Test before repeated exposure and verify calibration periodically.
Gases
  Air | O | No noticeable effect
  Ammonia | C,F,T | No noticeable effect
  Argon | A | No noticeable effect
  Carbon Dioxide | A | No noticeable effect
  Carbon Monoxide | F,T | No noticeable effect
  Chlorine | C,T | No noticeable effect
  Fluorine | C,O,T | No noticeable effect
  Helium | A | No noticeable effect
  Hydrogen | A,F | No noticeable effect
  Hydrogen Sulfide | C,F,T | No noticeable effect
  Methane | A,F | No noticeable effect
  Nitrogen | A | No noticeable effect
  Nitrous Oxide | O | No noticeable effect
  Ozone | O | No noticeable effect
  Propane | A,F | No noticeable effect
Oxidants
  Hydrogen Peroxide | O | No noticeable effect
  Sodium Hypochlorite | O | No noticeable effect at low concentrations and room temperature. Moderate to severe effect at high concentration and/or high temperature. Test before using and verify calibration periodically.

specifications.Sodium Hydroxide_effect: Minor effect or a slight change in appearance or properties. Test before repeated exposure and verify calibration periodically.
specifications.Sodium Hypochlorite_effect: No noticeable effect at low concentrations and room temperature. Moderate to severe effect at high concentration and/or high temperature. Test before using and verify calibration periodically.

key_facts: Most listed caustics (Ammonium Hydroxide, Potassium Hydroxide), all listed gases, and Hydrogen Peroxide have no noticeable effect on the material.
Sodium Hydroxide may cause minor effects or a slight change in appearance or properties and requires periodic calibration verification and testing before repeated exposure.
Sodium Hypochlorite shows no noticeable effect at low concentrations and room temperature but can have moderate to severe effects at high concentrations and/or high temperatures.
For Sodium Hypochlorite, testing before use and periodic calibration verification are required.
warnings_notes: For Sodium Hydroxide: Test before repeated exposure and verify calibration periodically.
For Sodium Hypochlorite: Test before using and verify calibration periodically. Note potential moderate to severe effect at high concentration and/or high temperature.

chunks.page_summary: This page from a technical document describes commonly used cleaners, focusing on alkaline and caustic (sodium hydroxide) cleaning for Rheonics probes and sensors, including details on the corrosion resistance and stress corrosion cracking susceptibility of stainless steel 316.
chunks.extracted_text:
Figure 2. Chemical compatibility table[2]

4. Commonly used cleaners

4.1. Alkaline cleaning
Alkaline solutions can be applied to the Rheonics probe to remove water-soluble materials. Alkaline solutions dissolve the bonds between water-soluble molecules, allowing them to be drawn off the probe and away from the surface, keeping the probe clean.

4.2. Caustic (Sodium Hydroxide)
Sodium Hydroxide (Caustic Soda) is a strong base.
In a wide range of industries including investment casing, it is used for metal degreasing and cleaning.
Rheonics sensors made of stainless steel 316 are considered resistant to corrosion below 80 degrees Celsius, up to the limit of their solubility.

[Graph: Corrosion resistance of stainless steels]

Figure 3. Corrosion resistance of stainless steels[3]

Up to the limit of solubility, they are resistant to sodium hydroxide at any temperature below 80 °C.
Stainless steel 316 material is susceptible to stress corrosion cracking (SCC) attack at higher temperatures. If service temperatures are limited to 95°C, this should not be an issue.

4.3. Acidic cleaning
key_facts: Alkaline solutions can be used on Rheonics probes to remove water-soluble materials by dissolving intermolecular bonds.
Sodium Hydroxide (Caustic Soda) is a strong base used in industries for metal degreasing and cleaning.
Rheonics sensors made of stainless steel 316 are resistant to corrosion by sodium hydroxide below 80 degrees Celsius.
Stainless steel 316 is susceptible to stress corrosion cracking (SCC) at higher temperatures.
Limiting service temperatures to 95°C for stainless steel 316 should prevent stress corrosion cracking (SCC).
diagram_labels: Figure 3. Corrosion resistance of stainless steels[3]
warnings_notes: Stainless steel 316 material is susceptible to stress corrosion cracking (SCC) attack at higher temperatures.
